Claim ID: claim:llm_productivity:taskfit:001
Retrieved: 2026-05-27

Source: Xiao Pu, Mingqi Gao, Xiaojun Wan. "Summarization is (Almost) Dead." arXiv:2309.09558, September 2023.

URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.09558

Excerpt:
The paper evaluates the zero-shot generation capability of LLMs across five distinct
summarization tasks (single-news, multi-news, dialogue, source code, cross-lingual).

"Summaries generated by the LLMs consistently outperform both human and summaries
generated by fine-tuned models across all tasks."

"Human-written reference summaries exhibit either an equal or higher number of
hallucinations compared to GPT-4 summaries."

The authors examined 100 recent summarization papers from ACL, EMNLP, NAACL, and COLING
and found that approximately 70% focused on proposing methods validated against standard
datasets -- work the authors suggest is becoming less relevant given LLM capabilities.

Evaluations used pairwise comparisons with human annotators across 50-sample datasets
per task, employing "WinRate" metrics to quantify relative system performance.

Recency note: This is a September 2023 paper. It falls under recency category 3
(conceptual foundation -- what LLMs can do well at summarization) and remains the
canonical reference for LLM summarization superiority. The finding has been reinforced
by subsequent 2024-2025 surveys (e.g., ACM Systematic Survey of Text Summarization,
doi:10.1145/3731445).
